message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The
                    <jats:italic>cation/proton exchanger</jats:italic>
                    (
                    <jats:italic>CHX</jats:italic>
                    ),
                    <jats:italic>salt overly sensitive</jats:italic>
                    (
                    <jats:italic>SOS</jats:italic>
                    ),
                    <jats:italic>and receptor-like kinase</jats:italic>
                    (
                    <jats:italic>RLK</jats:italic>
                    ) genes play significant roles in the response to salt stress in plants. This study is the first to identify the
                    <jats:italic>SOS</jats:italic>
                    gene in
                    <jats:italic>Solanum lycopersicum</jats:italic>
                    (tomato) through genome-wide analysis under salt stress conditions. Quantitative reverse transcription PCR (qRT-PCR) results indicated that the expression levels of
                    <jats:italic>CHX</jats:italic>
                    ,
                    <jats:italic>SOS</jats:italic>
                    , and
                    <jats:italic>RLK</jats:italic>
                    genes were upregulated, with fold changes of 1.83, 1.49, and 1.55, respectively, after 12 h of exposure to salt stress. Genome-wide analysis revealed 21
                    <jats:italic>CHX</jats:italic>
                    , 5
                    <jats:italic>SOS</jats:italic>
                    , and 86
                    <jats:italic>RLK</jats:italic>
                    genes in
                    <jats:italic>S. lycopersicum</jats:italic>
                    .
                    <jats:italic>CHX</jats:italic>
                    genes were found on chromosomes 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 11, and 12 of
                    <jats:italic>S. lycopersicum</jats:italic>
                    .
                    <jats:italic>SOS</jats:italic>
                    genes were found on chromosomes 1, 4, 6, and 10.
                    <jats:italic>RLK</jats:italic>
                    genes were found on all chromosomes of
                    <jats:italic>S. lycopersicum</jats:italic>
                    . The Ka/Ks ratios indicate that the
                    <jats:italic>CHX</jats:italic>
                    ,
                    <jats:italic>SOS</jats:italic>
                    , and
                    <jats:italic>RLK</jats:italic>
                    genes have been primarily influenced by purifying selection. This suggests that these genes have faced strong environmental pressures throughout their evolution. Purifying selection typically results in a decrease in genetic diversity. The estimated duplication time for
                    <jats:italic>CHX</jats:italic>
                    paralogous gene pairs ranged from approximately 26.965 to 245.413 million years ago (Mya), while the duplication time for
                    <jats:italic>SOS</jats:italic>
                    paralogous gene pairs ranged from around 116.682 to 275.631 Mya. For
                    <jats:italic>RLK</jats:italic>
                    paralogous gene pairs, the duplication time varied from approximately 27.689 to 239.376 Mya. Synteny analysis of the
                    <jats:italic>CHX</jats:italic>
                    ,
                    <jats:italic>SOS</jats:italic>
                    , and
                    <jats:italic>RLK</jats:italic>
                    genes demonstrated collinear relationships with orthologous genes in
                    <jats:italic>Arabidopsis thaliana</jats:italic>
                    , but no collinearity orthologous relationships in
                    <jats:italic>Oryza sativa</jats:italic>
                    (rice). Furthermore, the analysis revealed that there were 6 orthologous
                    <jats:italic>SlCHX</jats:italic>
                    genes, 2 orthologous
                    <jats:italic>SlSOS</jats:italic>
                    genes, and 44 orthologous
                    <jats:italic>SlRLK</jats:italic>
                    genes paired with those in A. thaliana. The results of the present study may help to elucidate the role of the
                    <jats:italic>CHX</jats:italic>
                    ,
                    <jats:italic>SOS</jats:italic>
                    , and
                    <jats:italic>RLK</jats:italic>
                    genes in salt stress in
                    <jats:italic>S. lycopersicum</jats:italic>
                    .
                  </jats:p>
